founder.title: The Founder
founder.text: I'm Sergio Brotons: founder of DigitalCoa.ch. After 15 years helping leaders navigate digital transformation, governance challenges, and strategic complexity, I kept seeing the same pattern: the problem was rarely a lack of tools or data. It was a lack of decision clarity.

Insights.title: Insights
Insights.subtitle: Strategic perspectives on AI governance and decision systems.

personal-cognitive-os.title: For Professionals: A Personal Cognitive Operating System
personal-cognitive-os.excerpt: How to build a personal environment that preserves judgment and clarity in a world of algorithmic noise.
personal-cognitive-os.content: A cognitive operating system isn't about collecting tools; it's about protecting attention. As a senior professional, your judgment is your core asset. If your environment is fragmented, so is your judgment.
list: Minimal toolset: Only tools that serve thinking.
Localized caches: Keep current context instantly available.
Routine automation with checkpoints: Automate process, not judgment.
Decision logs: Track why decisions were made, not just the outcome.
Weekly tidy ritual: Regularly clear the cognitive workspace.

collective-judgment.title: For Teams: Architectures that Amplify Collective Judgment
collective-judgment.excerpt: Designing team environments that reduce noise and focus on strategic judgment.
collective-judgment.content: Collaboration without structure generates noise. Teams need architectural boundaries to separate execution updates from strategic debate. When the lines blur, decisions are delayed by consensus-seeking.
list: Single source of truth: One unified hub for reality.
Role clarity: Define who decides vs. who advises.
Lightweight rituals: Meetings with strict, protective agendas.
Local error budgets: Allow decentralized execution safely.
Compact documentation: Write to be understood quickly, not exhaustively.

oversight-vs-execution.title: For Boards: Frameworks that Separate Oversight from Execution
oversight-vs-execution.excerpt: Governance structures that ensure accountability without micro-management.
oversight-vs-execution.content: A board's role is oversight, not operation. When governance frameworks are weak, boards accidentally drift into management. Strong decision architecture builds a clear moat around executive action.
list: Rule-based escalation: Clear triggers for when the board must step in.
Auditable trails: Transparent logic for executive actions.
Boundary contracts: Agreed limits on executive autonomy.
Metric-first reporting: Let the data speak before the narrative.
Renewal and sunset: Regular reviews of existing policies.

swissdamed-13-weeks-medical-practices.title: Swissdamed in 13 Weeks: What Small Medical Practices Actually Need to Do
swissdamed-13-weeks-medical-practices.excerpt: Swissmedic confirmed no grace period for high-risk AI medical devices. Small medical practices face Swissdamed + nFADP obligations with a hard July 1, 2026 deadline.
swissdamed-13-weeks-medical-practices.content: Thirteen weeks is not a lot of time. It's enough — but only if you stop treating Swissdamed as something to think about later. Swissmedic has confirmed there is no grace period for high-risk medical devices with serious incident reporting obligations. The July 1, 2026 date is not a target. It's a line. If your practice uses AI-assisted diagnostic imaging, AI-based symptom triage, or any AI tool that processes patient data, you fall under Swissdamed's scope. The question is not whether you have obligations. You do. The question is whether you have a plan. Most small practices have no formal AI governance policy, no inventory of AI tools in use, no documented review process, and no Swissmedic compliance pathway. This is the gap between what's happening in your practice and what Swissmedic expects. The 30/60/90-day sequence: by July 1, inventory and classify every AI tool in use; by August 1, assess and document conformity assessment status; by September 1, implement and maintain a governance policy with named accountability.
list: High-risk AI medical devices (Class IIb+): Conformity assessment before market placement, post-market surveillance, serious incident reporting to Swissmedic.
Lower-risk AI tools: nFADP obligations if patient data is processed, general safety requirements, documentation obligations.
All AI tools processing patient data: nFADP Art. 5(c) data controller obligations are active since September 2023.
FDPIC healthcare enforcement priority: practices without documented AI governance are increasingly exposed to regulatory action.
The critical test: does the AI tool have a medical purpose — diagnosis, treatment, monitoring? If yes, Swissdamed applies.

46-point-gap-small-law-firms.title: The 46-Point Gap: Why Small Law Firms Can't Wait on AI Governance
46-point-gap-small-law-firms.excerpt: 69% of legal professionals use gen AI individually. Only 23% of firms with fewer than 10 employees have formal AI policies. FDPIC is building an AI enforcement portfolio. The gap is your exposure.
46-point-gap-small-law-firms.content: There is a 46-point gap between how many legal professionals use generative AI and how many their firms have policies for. 69% use it individually. 23% of firms with fewer than 10 employees have formal AI data rules. That gap is not a statistic. It is an exposure. FDPIC has opened multiple AI enforcement actions in 2025 and 2026 — transport companies, retailers, social media platforms. None targeting law firms yet. This is not an exemption. It is a sequencing decision. When FDPIC turns to professional services, firms with active Shadow AI use and no documented governance framework will be the most exposed. Three overlapping pressure events for Geneva law firms in 2026: nFADP operative since September 2023, EU AI Act high-risk obligations activating August 2, 2026, and the Federal Council AI Action Plan First Draft milestone at Digital Gipfel Andermatt on May 26, 2026. The practical starting point: document what AI is being used, by whom, and on what data. This is your AI inventory. It is the foundation of everything else.
46-point-gap-small-law-firms.listTitle: The three-decision framework for law firms:
list: Decision 1 — Are you using AI? If any attorney or paralegal uses AI for research, drafting, or review, nFADP obligations are active.
Decision 2 — Are you processing client data with AI? If yes, DPIA may be required under nFADP Art. 22.
Decision 3 — Do you have EU AI Act exposure? If your firm has EU-based clients or operates AI systems with EU-intended outputs, August 2, 2026 is your high-risk deadline.
FDPIC enforcement portfolio: 4 confirmed AI investigations 2025-2026; law firms not yet targeted, but the pattern is established.
First-mover advantage: there are no Geneva-area AI governance boutiques serving small law firms.

carf-live-wealth-managers.title: CARF Is Live: What Geneva Wealth Managers Need to Know About AI Governance Right Now
carf-live-wealth-managers.excerpt: CARF operative since Jan 1, 2026. FATF evaluation active. 76% of Geneva FS firms use AI. Three compliance layers for wealth managers. Here is what to do right now.
carf-live-wealth-managers.content: CARF has been operative since January 1, 2026. That is not a future event. It is not a consultation document. It is live regulatory infrastructure generating reporting obligations for crypto-asset transactions — whether your firm is ready or not. The FATF mutual evaluation of Switzerland is also active, with evaluators in-market and asking questions about AML/KYC AI systems. And underneath both: nFADP obligations for any AI processing client personal data that have been operative since September 2023. Three overlapping compliance layers. One firm. One window to get organized. CARF changes the AI governance conversation because if your firm uses AI tools to process, analyze, or report on crypto assets, CARF obligations are active and auditable. If those same AI tools process client personal data, nFADP layers on top. If the firm has EU-nexus clients or infrastructure, add the EU AI Act. Most boutique firms don't see this stacking. The reality: it is one governance problem with multiple regulatory faces.
carf-live-wealth-managers.listTitle: The 3-layer compliance stack for boutique wealth managers:
list: Layer 1 — nFADP (Swiss domestic): Any AI tool processing client personal data requires lawful basis, purpose limitation, and data subject rights processes. DPIA required for high-risk AI.
Layer 2 — FINMA + FATF + CARF (Swiss international): AI systems used in AML/KYC, client suitability, or investment advice must be documented and auditable.
Layer 3 — EU AI Act (extraterritorial, if applicable): GPAI obligations active since August 2, 2025. High-risk obligations activate August 2, 2026 for firms with EU-nexus.
FATF evaluation: evaluators will ask about governance frameworks behind AI use in AML/KYC, client screening, and suitability assessment.
The practical starting point: document every AI tool currently in use — including personal devices and unofficial tools.

67-percent-real-estate-ai-governance.title: 67% of Geneva Real Estate Agencies Use AI. Most Have No Governance Framework.
67-percent-real-estate-ai-governance.excerpt: 67% AI adoption in Geneva real estate — highest of any professional vertical. GAIGI is being implemented. nFADP is active. The retroactive compliance path for agencies that have already adopted AI.
67-percent-real-estate-ai-governance.content: 67% of Geneva real estate agencies are using AI tools. That is the highest adoption rate of any professional vertical in the city. The governance frameworks are not keeping pace. Most agencies have no formal AI policy, no inventory of what tools are in use, no documented accountability structure for AI-assisted outputs. This is the retroactive compliance problem. It is not a future tense issue. It is the current state of most of the market. Real estate agencies are using AI for property listing descriptions and marketing copy, client communication drafting, market analysis and comparable property research, administrative task automation, and CRM. Each of these involves data — some involves personal data. GAIGI — the Geneva Real Estate Agent Professional Regulation — is being implemented. nFADP has been operative since September 2023. For agencies with EU market activity, the EU AI Act adds a further layer. The retroactive compliance path has four steps: inventory your AI tools, classify by data sensitivity, establish named accountability, and set a review cycle.
67-percent-real-estate-ai-governance.listTitle: Where AI lives in a Geneva real estate agency:
list: Marketing and listing: AI tools drafting property descriptions, generating marketing copy, optimizing listing text.
Client communication: AI-assisted drafting of client emails, follow-up sequences, meeting preparation summaries.
Market research: AI tools processing comparable property data, market trend analysis, neighbourhood profiling.
Administrative automation: Scheduling, calendar management, CRM updates, document organization.
GAIGI is being implemented — professional practice standards in active development, AI governance standards expected to follow.

gpai-obligations-consulting-firms.title: GPAI Obligations Have Been Active for 8 Months. Most Small Consulting Firms Haven't Noticed.
gpai-obligations-consulting-firms.excerpt: EU AI Act GPAI obligations operative since Aug 2, 2025. Federal Council First Draft May 26, 2026. Dual compliance for Geneva consulting firms with EU nexus.
gpai-obligations-consulting-firms.content: The EU AI Act's GPAI transparency obligations became operative on August 2, 2025. That is 8 months ago. If your Geneva consulting firm serves EU-based clients, uses AI tools with EU-based data infrastructure, or produces outputs intended for the EU market, those obligations have been applying to your GPAI providers — and indirectly, to your firm — since that date. GPAI covers foundation models and general-purpose AI systems. Most commercial AI tools used in consulting fall into this category. The operative GPAI obligations require providers to maintain technical documentation, publish training data summaries including copyright compliance, implement acceptable use policies, and maintain incident response processes. For consulting firms, you must disclose when content you deliver is AI-generated, and you must have an acceptable use policy. The Federal Council's AI Action Plan reaches its First Draft milestone at Digital Gipfel Schweiz Andermatt on May 26, 2026. When it does, the Geneva consulting community will have a collective conversation about AI governance. The firms that already have a framework will be the reference points.
list: Axis 1 — GPAI Transparency (EU AI Act): If your firm serves EU clients or processes EU-based data, GPAI obligations are active. Disclose AI-generated content, maintain technical documentation.
Axis 2 — nFADP Domestic Compliance: If AI tools process personal client project data, Art. 5(c) data controller obligations are active. DPIA may be required for high-risk processing.
Axis 3 — Client Expectation Management: Institutional clients increasingly ask about AI use in project delivery. A documented AI use policy is becoming a contract requirement.
GPAI obligations since Aug 2, 2025: 8 months already operative. No grace period.
May 26, 2026 Digital Gipfel Andermatt: Federal Council AI Action Plan First Draft — will trigger market-wide awareness spike.

geneva-ai-governance-portfolio.title: The 2026 Geneva AI Governance Portfolio: What I Found Across Six Professional Verticals
geneva-ai-governance-portfolio.excerpt: After 30+ research cycles across six Geneva verticals: high AI adoption, low governance everywhere. Swissdamed hard deadline ~13 weeks. CARF operative. No boutique competitors. First-mover window open.
geneva-ai-governance-portfolio.content: Over the past several months, I ran a continuous market research program across six Geneva professional services verticals. The objective was simple: understand what AI governance actually looks like for small firms in Geneva — where the adoption gaps are, where the regulatory pressure is real, and where the advisory opportunity exists. Pattern 1: High adoption, low governance, everywhere. Across law, wealth management, healthcare, real estate, consulting, and independent advisory, 61-76% of firms in each vertical are using AI tools. 23-42% have formal governance policies. The gap between adoption and accountability is the defining compliance exposure of 2026. Pattern 2: Regulatory deadlines are real and concrete. Swissdamed has a hard July 1 deadline with no grace period. CARF has been operative since January 1. GPAI obligations have been active since August 2, 2025. FATF evaluators are in-market. Pattern 3: No boutique competitor serving small firms. After more than thirty consecutive research cycles, I found no Geneva-area advisory boutique offering AI governance services specifically to small professional firms. Pattern 4: The first-mover window is open. The combination of high adoption, low governance, concrete deadlines, and no boutique competitors defines an open window.
list: Healthcare (Swissdamed Jul 1, ~13 weeks): Strongest urgency hook. No grace period. Most time-pressured ICP across the entire portfolio.
Legal (46-point governance gap): FDPIC enforcement portfolio building. 69% individual gen AI use vs 23% formal firm policies.
Wealth Management (CARF + FATF operative): 76% Geneva FS AI adoption. CARF generating obligations since Jan 1. FATF evaluators in-market.
Real Estate (67% AI adoption, highest vertical): Retroactive compliance framing. Lower-friction entry for RE agencies already using AI.
Consulting (GPAI active since Aug 2, 2025): 8 months of operative obligations. May 2026 Digital Gipfel as market awareness trigger.

hero.title: Your AI governance is not ready for July 1.
hero.subtitle: And the window to fix it is closing.
hero.description: Swissdamed, AMLA, LETA, EU AI Act. Four regulatory deadlines converging on Geneva firms in 2026. Most organizations are not prepared. I help leaders build the structures that keep decisions clear when compliance pressure peaks.

regulatory.title: The Geneva regulatory cluster: 2026
regulatory.note: These deadlines are not isolated. They overlap, reinforce, and create compliance pressure across every Geneva firm that uses AI in any form. The organizations that act first will define the standard.

swissdamed.title: Swissdamed: Healthcare
swissdamed.desc: Federal Act on the Exchange of Medical Data. Mandatory AI-assisted data processing registration. No grace period confirmed.

aml.title: AMLA: Finance
aml.desc: Anti-Money Laundering Act revision. AI-assisted transaction monitoring brings new compliance obligations for wealth management and banking-adjacent firms.

leta.title: LETA: Finance
leta.desc: Legal Entity Identifier Act. Corporate transparency requirements with AI governance implications for entity structures and decision accountability.

gpai.title: EU AI Act GPAI
gpai.desc: General Purpose AI transparency obligations already operative. Firms with EU clients or operations subject now: penalties up to EUR 35M.
